meetings.raw_text: NOTICE City of Fredericksburg Electoral Board will meet to administer their duties regarding the August 4, 2026 Republican Primary Election August 4, 2026, 4:30am-10:00 pm: For the administration of their Election Day duties. August 5, 2026, 12:00 pm: In the Office of the General Registrar and Director of Elections at 601 Caroline Street, Suite 500, for the purpose of canvassing the votes from election day for all precincts, results from absentee by mail and absentee by person. August 10, 2026, 10:00 am: In the Office of the General Registrar and Director of Elections at 601 Caroline Street, Suite 500, for the purpose of the Provisional Canvass of Votes in a limited open session meeting. Immediately following the Provisional Canvass, the Canvass of the post-election vote will occur. Once convened, the Electoral Board will remain in session, and will recess from time-to-time, for a date and time certain, to complete the requirements of the election and to properly conduct and conclude other business required by the Code of the Commonwealth or the State Department of Elections, in finalizing the election, the fiscal year, and any old or unfinished business from this and prior meetings. Portions of this meeting – beginning Tuesday, August 4, 2026, until their final adjournment – may include Closed Sessions as allowed by VFOIA §2.2-3711(A)(1, 7 & 8) and/or §24.2- 653. DIRECTOR OF ELECTIONS ELECTORAL BOARD & GENERAL REGISTRAR , Chair Jessica B. Atkinson G. Scott Walker, Vice Chair Marianna Bedway, Secretary 601 Caroline Street Suite 500 Fredericksburg, VA 22401 Email: vote@vote.fredericksburgva.gov Web: www.fredericksburgVA.gov/Vote Telephone: 540-372-1030 Fax: 540-373-8381

Written comments received on time will be read aloud during the public hearing. These rules apply to public comments: the person must identify themself by name and address, including zip code, limit remarks to five (5) minutes for speakers attending in person and three (3) minutes for submitted letters (read aloud), and comments must address a topic of City business. Decorum in the Council Chambers will be maintained. Comments that are not relevant to City business and behavior that is disruptive are inappropriate and out of order. A. video_clips.section_title: DSS Lease for Social Services Expansion
video_clips.summary: Council unanimously approves a lease for Department of Social Services to expand into 608 Jackson Street, addressing the growing need for space to serve vulnerable residents, with the city contributing approximately $50,000 annually.

A long-time resident and retired teacher speaks passionately against the proposed 1500 Gateway data center, citing noise, traffic, and environmental concerns, and urging the council to prioritize citizens over corporate interests.

Fred 15 advocates for reducing parking minimums for new affordable housing construction, arguing that current requirements add $10,000-$50,000 to costs and burden small infill lots, while supporting the planning department's proposal to streamline development.

Council unanimously supports FXB Go's application for state and federal transit funding to maintain safe operations and extend the life of service vehicles, ensuring continued public transit access for residents.

video_clips.section_title: Police Body Camera AI & License Plate Reader Policy
video_clips.summary: Police Chief Mason details the 'AI era bundle' for body cameras, highlighting real-time translation for 57 languages to aid crisis interactions and automated report drafting to get officers back on patrol. She also defends the use of license plate readers for recovering stolen vehicles.

meetings.raw_text: 601 Caroline Street, Suite 303 (540) 372-1216 Fredericksburg, VA 22401 (540) 372-6587 Fax Monday, July 13, 2026 8:30 a.m. ● EDA Regular Meeting FXBG City Center, First Floor Visitor Center Conference Room 601 Caroline St. Fredericksburg, VA 22401 1. Call to Order – Chair Schatz 2. Determination of Quorum 3. Approval of the Agenda 4. Public Comments 5. Approval of Minutes Special Meeting Minutes of June 16, 2026 6. Treasurer’s Report EDA Budget Update June 2026 7. Old Business Serve and Stay Fredericksburg Program Discussion – Chair Schatz 8. New Business 9. Committee & Liaison Reports a. City Relations Committee – Meredith Schatz b. InvestFXBG Loan Committee – Anita Crossfield & Evan Sullivan c. Parking Improvement – Kevin Hughes & Eugeniy Mikityuk d. Strategic Investments – Evan Sullivan e. Workforce Development – Meredith Schatz f. Tourism – Anita Crossfield & Eugeniy Mikityuk g. Main Street – Meredith Schatz h. Marketing & Promotions – Eugeniy Mikityuk i. Business Relations – Meredith Schatz & Anita Crossfield 10. Staff Report 11. Chair’s Report 12. Board Member Comments 13. Adjournment – Next Meeting, August 10, 2026 NOTE: THIS IS A TENTATIVE AGENDA AND SUBJECT TO ADJUSTMENTS PRIOR TO JULY 13, 2026. ANY CITIZEN THAT WANTS TO RECEIVE AN ELECTRONIC AGENDA MAY SIGN UP WITH THE REQUEST TO RECEIVE ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT AUTHORITY AGENDAS FORM. 601 Caroline Street, Suite 303 (540) 372-1216 Fredericksburg, VA 22401 (540) 372-6587 Fax ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT AUTHORITY (EDA) MINUTES Tuesday, June 16, 2026 8:30 a.m. ● Special Meeting FXBG City Center, First Floor Visitor Center Conference Room 601 Caroline St. Fredericksburg, VA 22401 The Economic Development Authority of the City of Fredericksburg, Virginia met in Special Session on Tuesday, June 16, 2026, at 8:30 a.m. at the FXBG City Center, Visitor Center Conference Room, 601 Caroline St., Fredericksburg, VA 22401. EDA MEMBERS PRESENT. Meredith Schatz, Chair presiding. Anita Crossfield, Tom Wack, Mitzi Brown, Eugeniy Mikityuk (via Teams), and Evan Sullivan (via Teams). ABSENT. Kevin Hughes. ALSO PRESENT. Susan N. Sargent, Resident; Jon Gerlach, Former City Councilor; Parrish Snead Franklin Simpson, PLC: Michael Whitaker, Attorney; 300th Blue Ribbon Steering Committee: Beth Black, Chair; Synctivate: Madeline McMahon, Marketing and Business Development; Hyperbole: John Simms, Reporter; Fredericksburg Main Street: Kristen Reed, Assistant Director; EDA Counsel: Jackson Key; EDA Assistant, Jordan Seurattan, Department of Economic Development and Tourism: Josh Summits, Director; Amy Peregoy, Economic Development Manager. DETERMINATION OF QUORUM. Mitzi Brown. APPROVAL OF AGENDA. Mr. Summits requested New Business, item c. InvestFXBG Loan Review Committee Representation to be added to the agenda. MOTION by Mitzi Brown, seconded by Anita Crossfield, to approve the amended agenda: Ayes (6); Nays (0); Absent from Meeting (1 - Hughes). PUBLIC COMMENTS. “My name is Sue Sergeant. I live at 1318 Williams Street, 22401. In talking with neighbors, we have some concerns with the lack of information about the Serve and Stay Fund, which is proposed as a local workforce housing initiative designed to help city employees and educators afford to live in the community they serve. The EDA requires public comments to be sent in ahead of the meeting, and I wrote this public comment, and the agenda was showing and the meeting date was yesterday, and it was on the website. Since then, a packet PDF has been added to the city website with more details on this workforce housing initiative. Neighbors remain concerned if this $30,000 needs to be allocated fast because it has to be spent down by the close of this fiscal year on June 30th. Or is this just seed money already privately donated? Fairfax County has workforce housing programs. They're often admired because they do it right the first time. They collect baseline data for need, not anecdotal comments from friends, and do a review of the literature to study similar initiatives. Was any of this done by the Fredericksburg EDA? How are two members of the Serve and Stay Fund committee going to review and approve applications within a two-week time frame to ensure a quick turnaround for the applicants? Does EDA already have their list of potential applicants, and then later we'll get the language and implementation details to match these names? Fairfax County has two departments assigned to work housing, due to the higher population. But how can you expect two people, two EDA members, to process these applications using criteria, sensitive private information in the criteria? Such as one, the gross household income must be at or below 80% of the area median income. Two, it must remain below the program's maximum income limits. Three, the rent on the applicant's unit must be up to 150% of the fair market rent. To whom does the applicant recipient certify their income eligibility annually? Do stipends for coaching and summer school count as income?...[truncated]

f. Tourism – Anita Crossfield & Eugeniy Mikityuk
i. Business Relations – Meredith Schatz & Anita Crossfield
NOTE: THIS IS A TENTATIVE AGENDA AND SUBJECT TO ADJUSTMENTS PRIOR TO JULY 13, 2026. ANY CITIZEN THAT WANTS TO
RECEIVE AN ELECTRONIC AGENDA MAY SIGN UP WITH THE REQUEST TO RECEIVE ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT AUTHORITY
The Economic Development Authority of the City of Fredericksburg, Virginia met in Special Session on Tuesday,
June 16, 2026, at 8:30 a.m. at the FXBG City Center, Visitor Center Conference Room, 601 Caroline St.,
EDA MEMBERS PRESENT. Meredith Schatz, Chair presiding. Anita Crossfield, Tom Wack, Mitzi Brown,
Eugeniy Mikityuk (via Teams), and Evan Sullivan (via Teams).
ALSO PRESENT. Susan N. Sargent, Resident; Jon Gerlach, Former City Councilor; Parrish Snead Franklin
Simpson, PLC: Michael Whitaker, Attorney; 300th Blue Ribbon Steering Committee: Beth Black, Chair;
Synctivate: Madeline McMahon, Marketing and Business Development; Hyperbole: John Simms, Reporter;
Fredericksburg Main Street: Kristen Reed, Assistant Director; EDA Counsel: Jackson Key; EDA Assistant,
Jordan Seurattan, Department of Economic Development and Tourism: Josh Summits, Director; Amy Peregoy,
APPROVAL OF AGENDA. Mr. Summits requested New Business, item c. InvestFXBG Loan Review
Committee Representation to be added to the agenda.
MOTION by Mitzi Brown, seconded by Anita Crossfield, to approve the amended agenda: Ayes (6); Nays
PUBLIC COMMENTS. “My name is Sue Sergeant. I live at 1318 Williams Street, 22401. In talking with
neighbors, we have some concerns with the lack of information about the Serve and Stay Fund, which is proposed as
a local workforce housing initiative designed to help city employees and educators afford to live in the community
they serve. The EDA requires public comments to be sent in ahead of the meeting, and I wrote this public comment,
and the agenda was showing and the meeting date was yesterday, and it was on the website. Since then, a packet
PDF has been added to the city website with more details on this workforce housing initiative.
Neighbors remain concerned if this $30,000 needs to be allocated fast because it has to be spent down by the close
of this fiscal year on June 30th. Or is this just seed money already privately donated? Fairfax County has workforce
housing programs. They're often admired because they do it right the first time.
They collect baseline data for need, not anecdotal comments from friends, and do a review of the literature to study
similar initiatives. Was any of this done by the Fredericksburg EDA? How are two members of the Serve and Stay
Fund committee going to review and approve applications within a two-week time frame to ensure a quick
turnaround for the applicants? Does EDA already have their list of potential applicants, and then later we'll get the
language and implementation details to match these names? Fairfax County has two departments assigned to work
housing, due to the higher population.
But how can you expect two people, two EDA members, to process these applications using criteria, sensitive
private information in the criteria? Such as one, the gross household income must be at or below 80% of the area
median income. Two, it must remain below the program's maximum income limits. Three, the rent on the applicant's
unit must be up to 150% of the fair market rent.
To whom does the applicant recipient certify their income eligibility annually? Do stipends for coaching and
summer school count as income? Will the applicant sign and maintain a two-year lease? Will the applicant be a
contracted employee, a teacher, a bus driver, a recruit in training, and not a 1099 contractor? If the applicant receives
a housing choice voucher, formerly known as Section 8, are they given priority points? Is this fund meant for
applicants who are single adults over 18 years old, living on a limited budget? Does the EDA have an MOU,
Memorandum of Understanding with the schools and city police, fire, rescue, sheriff, that they will report in writing
within a week if their employee quits the contract? Do these agents, the agencies, have it in writing what EDA is
already expecting them to do? Who pays for the applicant's background verification, like credit checks, criminal
history review, and rental reference verifications? Is there an exclusion clause that the applicant cannot already own
residential real estate? Will the fund also pay for pet deposits or not? Has Kelly Lackey, City Attorney, looked at the
applicant criteria? Has she thought that there could be a disparate impact, indirect discrimination, by locking out
other professions in heightened fields, like ER nurses or surgical techs? Will there be a disclosure statement to the
applicant for informed consent in accepting the money? In fact, is it taxable income? Have you even communicated
to the local rental marketeers if they'll offer profession-based discounts on the lower security deposit, wave
application fees, or offer monthly rent discount exclusively for teachers and first responders? Kilburn Crossing near
Chancellor High School out in Spotsylvania County was doing this under their Hometown Heroes initiative in 2007,
irregardless of income. And I was able to benefit from it as a four-year-old teacher.
So anyway, those things are just the usual statements, I think, from the applicant. I certify that this information is
correct and complete to the best of my knowledge, and I give consent for exchange of information with the agencies,
which seems fine, but with the EDA members, I am concerned about their privacy and that they will notify the
EDA, promptly within a week in writing if any changes in the address, employment, or income.
Thank you for your consideration of these comments and questions”.
MOTION by Mitzi Brown seconded by Tom Wack for the regular meeting minutes from May 11, 2026 were
approved: Ayes (6); Nays (0); Absent from Meeting (1 - Hughes).
a.   Chair Schatz provided the Treasurer’s Report on Mr. Hughes’ behalf. Chair Schatz reported that in May,
significant financial activities included the settlement of legal fees related to ongoing EDA initiatives with
legal counsel, ongoing marketing promotion expenditures disbursed to Lori Rowe, and a partial payment to
WZHA, LLC for services rendered on the Phase III Corridor Study. The EDA also received payments from
multiple borrowers, all of whom are currently in good standing.
b.   FY2027 Budget Discussion - Members discussed revisions to the FY2027 budget. Legal expenditure
allocations have been reassessed to $14,000, following a collaborative review with Mr. Key and Mr.
Whitaker to optimize spending, and a strategic adjustment has been made to the marketing and promotions
budget. In consultation with the EDT staff, it was determined that the EDA′s financial support for Lori
Rowe′s services will be phased out. For FY27, the EDA will contribute $15,000, representing a 50%
reduction in the current contract rate, with the understanding that the EDT will assume full marketing
budget responsibility in subsequent fiscal years. This adjustment anticipates a further reduction in EDA's
marketing allocation in future years, absent specific EDA-directed marketing campaigns.
MOTION by Mitzi Brown seconded by Anita Crossfield to approve the FY2027 Budget as amended: Ayes
a.   Serve and Stay Fredericksburg Fund Program Discussion – Chair Schatz
Chair Schatz reported the agreement with the Community Foundation has been finalized, incorporating
numerous edits. The fund's parameters and guidelines can be modified with direct communication to Dr.
Members allocated $30,000 of EDA funds to establish the program, and the final stages of developing both
the grantee and funder pages on the Community Foundation's website are underway. The anticipated launch
date is by the end of June, specifically around the 29th or 30th, coinciding with the prime recruitment
season for teachers, police officers, and firefighters. The fund's primary purpose is to serve as a recruitment
and retention tool through housing incentives, rather than a housing affordability program.
Chair Schatz clarified that no recipients have been approved as the fund has not yet been launched. Efforts
are currently focused on securing additional private funding, with an initial $10,000 secured from the
business community, signaling broad community support. The Community Foundation's extensive
experience in administering similar funds, particularly through its CEO, Dr. Rodgers, who was instrumental
in designing the program. The next communication regarding the fund is expected to be a press release.
Members celebrated the initiative as a collaborative effort that began with informal conversations and has
evolved into a robust program designed to support individuals who wish to live and work in
a.   Election of Officers – Chair Schatz
The slate of officers, as previously proposed, is Meredith Schatz, Chair; Anita Crossfield, Vice Chair;
Kevin Hughes, Treasurer; and Eugeniy Mikityuk, Secretary.
MOTION by Tom Wack seconded by Mitzi Brown to accept the slate of officers, as presented: Ayes (6); Nays
b.   Resolution No. 26-06 - Authorizing the Chair of the EDA to execute an Economic Development
Agreement with 2300 Fall Hill Partners, LLC and the City of Fredericksburg for the Redevelopment of
the Property Commonly known as the Former Mary Washington Hospital – Mr. Summits
Mr. Summits highlighted that this agreement, which has been reviewed by legal counsel, previously
received a unanimous 7-0 vote at the City Council meeting on June 9th, with a second read scheduled for
June 23rd. Two minor amendments have been made: a grammatical correction on page four regarding the
"Projects" section and a revised definition of affordability targets. The updated language for affordability
now states incentives will be for "public sector employees, including Fredericksburg City School and City
of Fredericksburg employees” to apply, broadening the scope from the previous mention of teachers and
first responders. The incentive agreement secures 70 units within the 242-unit development for a 10-year
period, with 20% of units (12 studios) designated at 80% AMI for $1,000 per month. Qualifying public
sector employees will receive a security deposit of $500 on their first month's rent. The board expressed
excitement for this timely initiative, and it was clarified that 2300 Fall Hill Partners has significant
experience in managing such projects and will be responsible for informing the public about these
opportunities through their established intake processes. Annual reporting will ensure compliance. The
developers are currently in site plan review and historic tax credit due diligence, anticipating certification in
Q4 2028, with most units expected to come online around the same time.
MOTION by Mitzi Brown seconded by Eugeniy Mikityuk to approve Resolution No. 26-06 - Authorizing the
Chair of the EDA to execute an Economic Development Agreement with 2300 Fall Hill Partners, LLC and the
City of Fredericksburg for the Redevelopment of the Property Commonly known as the Former Mary
Washington Hospital: Ayes (6); Nays (0); Absent from Meeting (1 - Hughes).
Following the April approval of revised InvestFXBG loan programs and guidelines, the EDA sought to re-
establish banking sector representation on the Loan Review Committee (LRC). Following discussions with
various banks, three institutions have expressed interest in participating: Citizens and Farmers Bank (C&F),
First Citizens Bank, and Shore United Bank.
Initially, Shore United Bank intended to participate but have experienced staffing changes causing a delay
on their end. Shore United is currently identifying a new member to fill that spot. Daniel Maddox, formerly
with Shore United Bank and now a VP Senior Commercial Credit Officer at C&F Bank, has expressed
interest in serving on the LRC and brings extensive underwriting experience, making C&F Bank the second
participating institution. First Citizens Bank, known for its community involvement, has designated Samir
Patel, VP of Business Banking, as their representative. These three banks will form the banking
Regarding the timeline for loan reviews, it was confirmed that meetings will be regularly scheduled as
applications arise. The aim is to process recommendations from the LRC and present them to the full EDA
agenda within approximately one week, typically at the end of the month, by coordinating with committee
MOTION by Mitzi Brown seconded by Tom Wack to approve Citizens and Farmers Bank (C&F), Shore
United, and First Citizens Bank as members of the Loan Review Committee: Ayes (6); Nays (0); Absent from
a. City Relations Committee – Chair Schatz reported the City Relations Committee will meet in September.
b. InvestFXBG Loan Committee – Ms. Crossfield reported all accounts are up to date and there are no new
g. Main Street – Ms. Brown reported Main Street is running their ‘Downtown Summer Nights’ initiative
officially launched last Thursday and will run through August 22. This initiative features 26 shops open
later Thursdays from 5 p.m. to 8 p.m., 24 restaurants offering extended happy hours and specials, along
with street performers and yard games. Last week, Fredericksburg Main Street received a Milestone Award
from Virginia Main Street, presented by the Secretary of Commerce, Carrie Chenery. This award
recognized over $40 million in private investment in the Downtown District since Fredericksburg's

meetings.raw_text: Agenda - 07/08/2026 PLANNING COMMISSION AGENDA July 8, 2026 6:30 P.M. PLANNING COMMISSION MEETINGS CAN BE VIEWED ON THE FREDERICKSBURG GOVERNMENT ACCESS CHANNEL ON COX CHANNEL 84 AND VERIZON CHANNEL 42 and www.regionalwebtv.com/fredpc . 1. Call to Order 2. Pledge Of Allegiance 3. Determination of a Quorum 4. Approval of Agenda 4.A. Agenda Documents: 2026-07-08 Agenda.pdf 5. Discussion of Potential Policies, Ordinances or Applications 6. Declaration of Conflict of Interest and Disclosures of Contact 7. Old Business 8. Public Hearing Any person may speak at a public hearing for up to five minutes. An applicant in a land use case may give an opening presentation of the case for up to 15 minutes, regardless of the number of speakers the applicant chooses to give the presentation, and may give a response after all citizens have spoken, for up to five minutes, regardless of the number of speakers the applicant chooses to give the response. The Chairman may suspend these rules in order to adapt them to any special situation. Any written comments submitted on a hearing item must include the name, address, and zip code, of the commenter, and shall be submitted by (1) email to [email protected] , (2) U.S. Mail addressed to: 601 Caroline Street, Suite 400, Fredericksburg, VA 22401, or (3) delivered to the Community Planning and Building office located at 601 Caroline Street - 4th Floor by 2:30 PM the day of the meeting. Any written comments received will be forwarded to the Planning Commission and will be available for public inspection upon request. Public comments submitted during the meeting or through the Facebook Live streaming video will not be considered part of the official public comments of the meeting. Persons requiring accommodations to facilitate participation at the public hearing or have questions are encouraged to call Planning Services at 540-372-1179 (5 days prior for accommodations). 8.A. Area 8 Dixon Street / Mayfield Small Area Plan Comprehensive Plan Amendment and Zoning Map Amendment. COMPPLAN2605-0004 / ZMA2605-0014 Comprehensive Plan Amendment to Chapters 10 and 11 of the 2025 Comprehensive Plan to adopt a Small Area Plan for Planning Area 8 Zoning Map Amendment to rezone 44 parcels, encompassing approximately 33.14 acres of land located in Planning Area 8, currently zoned as Residential-4, I-1 Light Industrial, and Commercial Highway, to the Creative Maker District and to apply form-based regulations, as recommended in the draft Small Area Plan for Area 8 Documents: 1 Staff Memo - Area 8 Small Area Plan.pdf 2 Draft Small Area 8 Comprehensive Plan Amendment.pdf 3 Appendix 1- Changes Outside of Small Area 8.pdf 4 Zoning Map Amendment Map.pdf 5 Transect and Frontage Map.pdf 9. Other Business 10. General Public Comment A general public comment period is provided at each regular meeting for comments by citizens regarding any matter related to Commission business that is not listed on the agenda for Public Hearing. The Chair will request that speakers state their name, address and zip code; observe the five-minute time limit; and yield the floor when the Clerk indicates that their time has expired. No dialogue between speakers will be permitted. Citizens who wish to make general public comments without attending the meeting will be able to send their comments in writing by (1) delivering to the Community Planning and Building office located at 601 Caroline Street - 4th Floor, (2) U.S. Mail at 601 Caroline Street, Suite 400, Fredericksburg, VA 22401, or (3) email to [email protected] . Comments must be received by 2:30 p.m. the day of the meeting. In written public comments, the writer must identify themselves by name, address, and zip code. Such comments will be forwarded to the Planning Commission and will be available for public inspection upon request. Public comments submitted during the meeting or through the Facebook Live streaming video, will not be considered part of the official public comments of the meeting. 11. Approval Of Minutes 11.A. May 27, 2026 Draft Minutes Joint Work Session Documents: 2026-05-27 Draft Minutes Joint Work Session.pdf 11.B. June 10, 2026 Regular Meeting Draft Minutes Documents: 2026-06-10 Draft Minutes Regular Meeting.pdf 12. Commissioner Comments 12.A. Chairman Comments 12.B. Planning Commissioner Comments 13. Planning Director Comments 14. Adjournment The next Planning Commission Regular Meeting is scheduled for Wednesday, August 12, 2026, at 6:30 p.m. PLANNING COMMISSION MEETINGS CAN BE VIEWED ON THE FREDERICKSBURG GOVERNMENT ACCESS CHANNEL ON COX CHANNEL 84 AND VERIZON CHANNEL 42 and www.regionalwebtv.com/fredpc .

meetings.raw_text: AGENDA Electoral Board Meeting Tuesday, July 7, 2026, 5:00 pm Office of Voter Registration and Elections Fredericksburg City Center Public Speakers The Electoral Board provides this opportunity for comments from voters before the start of the meeting. Comments relevant to the registration of voters, the establishment of voting precincts, and polling places, and the conduct of the elections are welcome. To be fair to everyone, please observe the five-minute time limit and yield the floor when the Chair of the Electoral Board indicates that your time has expired. Observers At this point the Board will Open the Meeting. All speakers and those observers who have waived their right to speak are reminded that at this point you are clearly and only observers. You are not invited to interrupt nor attempt to engage Board members in discussion of Board Agenda items or other business. A. Public Comment B. Approval of the Agenda C. Approval of Minutes a. Electoral Board Meeting June 2, 2026 D. General Registrar Report E. Old Business a. August 4, 2026 Republican Primary 1. Establish Canvass Dates and Times F. New Business G. Closed Session H. Certification of Closed Session I. Approval of Officers of Election for August 4, 2026 Republican Primary J. Next Meeting Date K. Adjournment 601 Caroline Street Suite 500 Fredericksburg, VA 22401 Email: vote@vote.fredericksburgva.gov Web: www.fredericksburgVA.gov/Vote Telephone: 540-372-1030 Fax: 540-373-8381

2026-05-27.summary: This joint work session allows city officials to review feasibility scenarios and economic impacts for the Phase III Commercial Corridor Revitalization Study. The discussion focuses on how specific development models for shopping centers might affect the local economy and urban planning goals. No binding votes are taken during this informational session; it is designed to gather input before formal decisions are made.
2026-05-27.why: Changes to commercial corridors directly influence the city's tax base by determining which businesses can operate in designated zones. These zoning adjustments affect local traffic patterns, public safety resource allocation, and the availability of goods and services for residents. The study's outcomes will shape the physical character of neighborhoods and the economic opportunities available to local families.
2026-05-27.how: Residents can attend the meeting on May 27, 2026, at 5:30 PM in the Council Chambers at 715 Princess Anne Street. Public comment periods are typically available during work sessions; check the agenda posted 48 hours prior for specific sign-up procedures. You may also submit written comments via email to the City Clerk or your council member before the meeting date.

2026-06-02.summary: The provided raw agenda data is corrupted and unreadable, preventing identification of specific items for the Fredericksburg City Council session on June 2, 2026. Consequently, no factual summary of the meeting's decisions can be generated from this input.
2026-06-02.why: Without a valid agenda, it is impossible to determine how this specific session impacts local tax bases, zoning, schools, or public safety. Residents cannot assess the relevance of the meeting to their community without knowing the actual topics under discussion.
2026-06-02.how: To engage with the Fredericksburg City Council, residents should monitor the official city website for the finalized agenda prior to the June 2, 2026 meeting. Attend the session at the Council Chambers to observe proceedings, or submit public comments according to the city's established deadlines and procedures.

2026-05-26.summary: No specific agenda items are currently published for the Fredericksburg City Council session on May 26, 2026, as the detailed agenda has not yet been released. Typically, such sessions involve the consideration of ordinances, budget approvals, or policy updates affecting city operations. Residents should check the city's official website closer to the date for the finalized list of topics.
2026-05-26.why: City Council decisions directly impact local property values, zoning regulations, and the allocation of funds for public services like schools and infrastructure. Understanding the agenda helps residents anticipate changes that may affect their neighborhood's character, tax burden, and quality of life. Engaging with these decisions ensures that community priorities are reflected in municipal governance.
2026-05-26.how: Monitor the City of Fredericksburg's official website or contact the City Clerk's office for the published agenda prior to the meeting. Attend the session at the Council Chambers to observe proceedings and submit public comments during the designated time slots. Residents may also email council members with written input regarding specific agenda items once they are available.

2026-05-12.summary: The Fredericksburg City Council will conduct first and second readings of ordinances to adjust water, sewer, and EMS fees, approve a courthouse security budget, and finalize the FY 2027 real estate tax rate. The session also includes a public hearing on a national PFAS water settlement and appointments to the Arts Commission. These actions directly impact household utility costs, property taxes, and local infrastructure funding.
2026-05-12.why: Changes to water, sewer, and EMS fees will alter monthly household expenses for residents, while the real estate tax rate of $0.84 per $100 of assessed value affects property owners' annual tax liabilities. Approving the courthouse security budget impacts public safety resources, and the PFAS settlement may influence long-term water quality and community health. These financial decisions shape the city's fiscal health and service delivery for families and businesses.
